name: crispy-forms-foundation

metadata.name: crispy-forms-foundation
classifiers: Topic :: Software Development :: Libraries :: Python Modules
metadata.summary: Django application to add 'django-crispy-forms' layout objects for 'Foundation for sites'

modules.name: setup
documentation: !/usr/bin/env python

modules.name: crispy_forms_foundation.templatetags.crispy_forms_foundation_field
documentation:
Re implementation of `crispy_forms.templatetags.crispy_forms_field` needed to
have correct Foundation6 error class on input element.

functions.name: crispy_forms_foundation.templatetags.crispy_forms_foundation_field.crispy_field
documentation:
Override original template filter to use the `CrispyFoundationFieldNode`.

Usage is identical to the original one:

```
{% crispy_field field attrs %}
```

classes.name: crispy_forms_foundation.templatetags.crispy_forms_foundation_field.CrispyFoundationFieldNode
documentation:
Override original Node object to manage field in a proper way for Foundation
classes.

modules.name: crispy_forms_foundation.layout.grid
documentation:
References
    * `Foundation 6 Grid <https://get.foundation/sites/docs/grid.html>`_;

classes.name: crispy_forms_foundation.layout.grid.Row
documentation:
Wrap fields in a div whose default class is `row`. Example: ```python Row('form_field_1', 'form_field_2', 'form_field_3') ``` Act as a div container row, it will embed its items in a div like that: ```html
Content
```

classes.name: crispy_forms_foundation.layout.grid.RowFluid
crispy_forms_foundation.layout.grid.Column
documentation:
Wrap fields in a div. If not defined, CSS class will default to `large-12 columns`. `columns` class is always appended, so you don't need to specify it. This is the column from the Foundation Grid component, all columns should be contained in a **Row** or a **RowFluid** and you will have to define the column type in the `css_class` attribute. Example: ```python Column('form_field_1', 'form_field_2', css_class='small-12 large-6') ``` Will render to something like that: ```html
...
``` `columns` class is always appended, so you don't need to specify it. If not defined, `css_class` will default to `large-12`.

modules.name: crispy_forms_foundation.layout
documentation:
Layout items for Foundation components.

Inherits from the default **crispy_forms** layout objects to force templates on
the right `TEMPLATE_PACK` (defined from `settings.CRISPY_TEMPLATE_PACK`)
and implements Foundation components.

modules.name: crispy_forms_foundation.layout.fields

classes.name: crispy_forms_foundation.layout.fields.Field
documentation:
Layout object, contain one field name and you can add attributes to it
easily. For setting class attributes, you need to use `css_class`,
because `class` is a reserved Python keyword.

Example:

```python
Field('field_name', style="color: #333;", css_class="whatever",
      id="field_name")
```

classes.name: crispy_forms_foundation.layout.fields.FakeField
documentation:
Fake field is intended to be used with some app that does not honor field
ID on the input element alike `django-recaptcha` that build a textarea
with a dummy ID attribute. This leads to HTML validation error.

Fake field works as basic Field object except a `fake_field` variable is
passed to the template context.

Actually the only difference with a `Field` is label element drops
`for` attribute.

You should use this field in last resort.

classes.name: crispy_forms_foundation.layout.fields.Hidden
documentation:
Hidden field. Work as basic Field except the `hidden` value for `type`
attribute.

classes.name: crispy_forms_foundation.layout.fields.MultiField
documentation: MultiField container. Render to a MultiField

classes.name: crispy_forms_foundation.layout.fields.SplitDateTimeField
documentation:
Just an inherit from `crispy_forms.layout.Field` to have a common Field
for displaying field with the `django.forms.extra.SplitDateTimeWidget`
widget.

Simply use a specific template

classes.name: crispy_forms_foundation.layout.fields.InlineField
documentation:
Layout object for rendering an inline field with Foundation

Example:

```python
InlineField('field_name')
```

Or:

```python
InlineField('field_name', label_column='large-8',
            input_column='large-4', label_class='')
```

`label_column`, `input_column`, `label_class`, are optional argument.

classes.name: crispy_forms_foundation.layout.fields.InlineJustifiedField
documentation:
Same as InlineField but default is to be right aligned with a vertical
padding

classes.name: crispy_forms_foundation.layout.fields.SwitchField
documentation:
A specific field to use Foundation form switches

You must only use this with a checkbox field and this is a *raw* usage of
this Foundation element, you should see `InlineSwitchField` instead.

Example:

```python
SwitchField('field_name', style="color: #333;", css_class="whatever",
            id="field_name")
```

classes.name: crispy_forms_foundation.layout.fields.InlineSwitchField
documentation:
Like `SwitchField` it use Foundation form switches with checkbox field
but within an `InlineField`

Contrary to `SwitchField` this play nice with the label to be able to
display it (as Foundation form switches default behavior is to hide the
label text)

Example:

```python
InlineSwitchField('field_name')
```

Or:

```python
InlineSwitchField('field_name', label_column='large-8',
                  input_column='large-4', label_class='',
                  switch_class="inline")
```

`label_column`, `input_column`, `label_class`, `switch_class` are
optional argument.

modules.name: crispy_forms_foundation.layout.base
documentation:
References:
    * `Foundation 6 Callout <https://get.foundation/sites/docs/callout.html>`_;

classes.name: crispy_forms_foundation.layout.base.Div
documentation:
It wraps fields inside a `<div>` element.

You can set `css_id` for element id and `css_class` for a element
class names.

Example:

```python
Div('form_field_1', 'form_field_2', css_id='div-example',
    css_class='divs')
```

classes.name: crispy_forms_foundation.layout.base.Callout
documentation:
Act like `Div` but add a `callout` class name.

Example:

```python
Callout('form_field_1', 'form_field_2', css_id='div-example',
      css_class='divs')
```

modules.name: crispy_forms_foundation.layout.buttons

classes.name: crispy_forms_foundation.layout.buttons.ButtonHolder
documentation:
It wraps fields in an element `
`. This is where you should put Layout objects that render to form buttons like Submit. It should only hold `HTML` and `BaseInput` inherited objects. Example: ```python ButtonHolder( HTML(Information Saved), Submit('Save', 'Save') ) ```

classes.name: crispy_forms_foundation.layout.buttons.ButtonHolderCallout
documentation:
Act like `ButtonHolder` but add a `callout` class name on the main
`div`.

classes.name: crispy_forms_foundation.layout.buttons.ButtonGroup
documentation:
It wraps fields in an element `<div class="button-group">`.

This is where you should put Layout objects that render to form buttons
like Submit. It should only hold `HTML` and `BaseInput` inherited objects.

Example:

```python
ButtonGroup(
    Submit('Save', 'Save'),
    Button('Cancel', 'Cancel'),
)
```

classes.name: crispy_forms_foundation.layout.buttons.InputButton
documentation:
Used to create a Submit input descriptor for the {% crispy %} template tag:

```python
button = InputButton('Button 1', 'Press Me!')
```

:::{note}
The first argument is also slugified and turned into the id for
the button.
:::

classes.name: crispy_forms_foundation.layout.buttons.Button
documentation:
This is the old Button object that inherit from `InputButton` for
backward compatibility.

If you want to stand for an input button, you are invited to use
`InputButton` instead to avoid problem when `ButtonElement` will
become the new `Button` object.

classes.name: crispy_forms_foundation.layout.buttons.InputSubmit
documentation:
Used to create a Submit button descriptor for the {% crispy %} template
tag:

```python
submit = Submit('Search the Site', 'search this site')
```

classes.name: crispy_forms_foundation.layout.buttons.Submit
documentation:
This is the old Button object that inherit from `InputSubmit` for
backward compatibility.

If you want to stand for an input button, you are invited to use
`InputSubmit` instead to avoid problem when `ButtonSubmit` will
become the new `Submit` object.

classes.name: crispy_forms_foundation.layout.buttons.InputReset
documentation:
Used to create a Reset button input descriptor for the `{% crispy %}`
template tag:

```python
reset = Reset('Reset This Form', 'Revert Me!')
```

classes.name: crispy_forms_foundation.layout.buttons.Reset
documentation:
This is the old Button object that inherit from `InputReset` for
backward compatibility.

If you want to stand for an input button, you are invited to use
`InputReset` instead to avoid problem when `ButtonReset` will
become the new `Reset` object.

classes.name: crispy_forms_foundation.layout.buttons.ButtonElement
documentation: Contrary to `Button`, ButtonElement purpose use a `

classes.name: crispy_forms_foundation.layout.buttons.ButtonSubmit
documentation: Create a submit button following the `ButtonElement` behaviors: ```python button = ButtonSubmit('search', 'go-search', content="Search this site!") ```

classes.name: crispy_forms_foundation.layout.buttons.ButtonReset
documentation: Create a reset button following the `ButtonElement` behaviors: ```python button = ButtonReset('reset', 'revert' content="Revert Me!") ```

classes.name: crispy_forms_foundation.forms.FoundationFormMixin
documentation:
Mixin to implement a layout helper that will automatically build a form
layout.

Generally, you will prefer to use `FoundationForm` or
`FoundationModelForm` instead.

If you still want to directly use this mixin you'll just have to execute
`FoundationFormMixin.init_helper()` in your form init.

**Attributes**

title

: If set, defines the form's title

layout

: If set, override the default layout for the form

error_title

: Defines the error title for non field errors

form_id

: Defines the id of the form

classes

: Defines the classes used on the form

action

: Defines the action of the form. `reverse` will be called on the
  value. On failure the value will be assigned as is

method

: Defines the method used for the action

attrs

: Defines the attributes of the form

switches

: If True, will replace all fields checkboxes with switches

submit

: Adds a submit button on the form. Can be set to a Submit object or
  a string which will be used as the value of the submit button

title_templatestring

: Template string used to display form title (if any)

classes.name: crispy_forms_foundation.forms.FoundationForm
documentation:
A **Django form** that inherit from `FoundationFormMixin` to
automatically build a form layout

Example:

```python
from django import forms
from crispy_forms_foundation.forms import FoundationForm

class YourForm(FoundationForm):
    title = "Testing"
    action = 'test'
    layout = Layout(Fieldset("Section", "my_field", "my_field_2"))
    switches = False
    attrs = {'data_abide': ""}

    title = forms.CharField(label='Title', required=True)
    slug = forms.CharField(label='Slug', required=False)
```

classes.name: crispy_forms_foundation.forms.FoundationModelForm
documentation:
A **Django Model form** that inherit from `FoundationFormMixin` to
automatically build a form layout

Example:

```python
from crispy_forms_foundation.forms import FoundationModelForm

class YourForm(FoundationModelForm):
    title = "Testing"
    action = 'test'
    layout = Layout(Fieldset("Section", "my_field", "my_field_2"))
    switches = False
    attrs = {'data_abide': ""}

    class Meta:
        model = MyModel
        fields = ['my_field', 'my_field_2', 'my_field_3']
```

modules.name: crispy_forms_foundation.layout.containers

classes.name: crispy_forms_foundation.layout.containers.Fieldset
documentation:
It wraps fields in a `<fieldset>`:

```python
Fieldset("Text for the legend",
    'form_field_1',
    'form_field_2'
)
```

The first parameter is the text for the fieldset legend. This text is
context aware, so you can do things like :

```python
Fieldset("Data for {{ user.username }}",
    'form_field_1',
    'form_field_2'
)
```

classes.name: crispy_forms_foundation.layout.containers.Container
documentation:
Overrides original Container element to get the "active" classname from
Class attribute `active_css_class` so it's compatible with Foundation
5 and 6.

classes.name: crispy_forms_foundation.layout.containers.TabHolder

methods.name: crispy_forms_foundation.layout.containers.TabHolder.render
documentation:
Re-implement almost the same code from crispy_forms but passing
`form` instance to item `render_link` method.

Tabs holder object to wrap Tab item objects in a container:

```python
TabHolder(
    TabItem('My tab 1', 'form_field_1', 'form_field_2'),
    TabItem('My tab 2', 'form_field_3')
)
```

`TabHolder` direct children should allways be a `TabItem` layout item.

A random id is builded for the tab holder if you don't define it using
`css_id` argument.

The first `TabItem` containing a field error will be marked as
*active* if any, else this will be just the first `TabItem`.

classes.name: crispy_forms_foundation.layout.containers.VerticalTabHolder
documentation: VerticalTabHolder appends vertical class to TabHolder container

classes.name: crispy_forms_foundation.layout.containers.TabItem

methods.name: crispy_forms_foundation.layout.containers.TabItem.has_errors
documentation: Find tab fields listed as invalid

methods.name: crispy_forms_foundation.layout.containers.TabItem.render_link
documentation:
Render the link for the tab-pane. It must be called after render so
`css_class` is updated with `active` class name if needed.

Tab item object. It wraps fields in a div whose default class is "tabs" and
takes a name as first argument.

Tab item is also responsible of building its associated tab link with its
`render_link` using the `link_template` attribute.

Example:

```python
TabItem('My tab', 'form_field_1', 'form_field_2', 'form_field_3')
```

`TabItem` layout item has no real utility out of a `TabHolder`.

classes.name: crispy_forms_foundation.layout.containers.AccordionHolder

methods.name: crispy_forms_foundation.layout.containers.AccordionHolder.render
documentation:
Re-implement almost the same code from crispy_forms but using
`form` instance to catch field errors.

Accordion items holder object to wrap Accordion item objects in a
container:

```python
AccordionHolder(
    AccordionItem("group name", "form_field_1", "form_field_2"),
    AccordionItem("another group name", "form_field"),
)
```

`AccordionHolder` direct children should allways be a `AccordionItem`
layout item.

A random id is builded for the accordion holder if you don't define it
using `css_id` argument.

The first `AccordionItem` containing a field error will be marked as
*active* if any, else this will be just the first `AccordionItem`.

classes.name: crispy_forms_foundation.layout.containers.AccordionItem
documentation:
Accordion item object. It wraps given fields inside an accordion
tab. It takes accordion tab name as first argument.

The item name is also slugified to build an id for the tab if you don't
define it using `css_id` argument.

Example:

```python
AccordionItem("group name", "form_field_1", "form_field_2")
```
